costs: Coordination costs of establishing and maintaining inter-municipal cluster agreements
Capital cost of building conversion for multi-service use
Ongoing administrative overhead distinct from single-school administration
content.equity_impact: Could mitigate segregation risk relative to church-school transfer by keeping schools within the state/public system and combining resources, but the informal inter-municipal competition dynamic (F-live-school_network_planning-09) suggests risk of favoring settlements with stronger existing organizational capacity or lower Roma population share unless equity criteria are built into cluster-formation rules.
expected_benefits: Preserves community hub function of the school building even where standalone enrollment is sub-threshold
Enables cost-sharing of specialist teachers across a cluster, partially addressing the higher per-pupil cost documented for small schools
Provides an alternative to binary closure that Polish 2025 draft legislation and French clustering both demonstrate as functioning models
content.goal: Retain village primary schools as viable units by formally networking them into inter-municipal pedagogical clusters and/or converting them into multi-service hubs, drawing on French RPI-style clustering and Polish community-run and repurposing models.

implementation_steps.action: Identify micro-regional clusters of 2-4 village schools with combined enrollment sufficient to share specialist staff and establish formal cluster agreements
Apply existing rural development funding to convert non-classroom school space into co-located community/kindergarten services
implementation_steps.actor: Municipal governments and Magyar Falu Program administrators
implementation_steps.timeline: years 1-3, using existing program funding stream

implementation_steps.action: Issue regulatory guidance permitting multi-service co-location and shared cross-cluster staffing without triggering closure classification

mechanisms: Poland's 2025 draft legislation proposes repurposing small-school infrastructure for crèches and community services rather than binary closure, framing rural schools as social/cultural centres (F-live-polish_reform-06).

France's conventions ruralité and clustering logic enable co-financing and staffing allowances across cooperating municipalities, providing a template for shared-resource networks (F-live-international_comparison-03).

Local social capital and bonding/bridging networks were the determining factor in the one documented Hungarian case (Erdőkürt) of a saved school, indicating that formal networked structures could substitute for reliance on rare organic social capital (F-live-portuguese_reform-10, F-live-political_feasibility-03).

Kindergarten coverage gaps mirror primary-school access problems under the same enrollment-threshold framework, suggesting hub co-location with kindergarten services could jointly stabilize both under shared thresholds (F-live-hungarian_education_system-08).
risks: Success may remain uneven and dependent on local social capital, replicating the Erdőkürt pattern rather than producing uniform outcomes (F-live-portuguese_reform-10)
Informal inter-municipal competition over pupil 'pull' has been documented and could undermine voluntary clustering cooperation (F-live-school_network_planning-09)
Regulatory guidance alone (without funding guarantee) may be insufficient absent dedicated capital investment
content.title: Networked retention: inter-municipal school clusters and multi-service community hubs
